author: @wickedprotos1937
text: That light bulb he used  is a useful one. We got them on QVC a couple of years ago. They have an internal 18650 battery and is charged when screwed in like a regular bulb. When the power goes out they will stay on when the potential from the socket threads to center contact is low. That's why the conductive slight of hand works, he just shorts the base outer and center with the graphene. I broke the socket on one and added  a 18650 battery holder to the side. Very useful worklamp and charges in the 18650 charger, or screwed into a lamp( base also repaired).

author: @f.d.6667
text: Exploiting a "miracle material" to gaslight ordinary consumers/voters/investors is not a new thing at all: in "Black Mischief" (1932) author Evelyn Waugh explores how vitamins can be used to spice up a dinner menu to impress the guests of honor at a banquet by a small-scale potentate. In the 1950s "Atomic Burgers" were a meme used to "sell" nuclear power to the public ... and today we are believing in the powers of graphene and the blessings of the "low carbon" religion...

author: @mikejones-vd3fg
text: I gotta ask the obviouse, but you tried it? Then how do you know its fake?  Because they sell magic light bulbs, that dont even look like the one he's using....  Its not unreasonable to expect rubbing things together generate electricty, we found our first electricity this way - static.  And even if its a magic lightbulb, the light bulb is still able to generate electricty through friction, which is pretty cool anyhow.  Reminds me we still have crank generators, i wonder how long it would take to bicycle charge a 100ah battery compared to solar?

author: @matthewgreen1152
text: Robert, I gave great talk when the contest for the 3D printer started then never entered the competition. I came to the conclusion that someone else would have better use of it is the main reason. I did however complete the water gener... Converter, it was decent for a mach 1 design. It would only be of real use if built much larger. I'm in study across the board of engineering and energy conversation along with energy collection/storage as by necessity for the future. Time is my currency and knowledge will be what makes my survival easier in time ahead. If you had to make your own power station to run your homes electricity how you personally do it? Keep in mind I'm using anything I can obtain that is thrown to the curb by people who don't know how to fix things and can just go buy another. I find the starting point is directing of the rest, how little can you use at the beginning, this is separate from the first question being my most important to get from your wisdom and knowledge. Thank you for all the time into your channel as you have helped me advance greatly, please don't stop.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you ask some very good questions mate - and the answers are more than I can reasonably write her - so - of course - I can do a video n this topic if it would help - cheers, Rob

replies.author: @ColRubyDimples
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yay! This is a great question, and one I've been wanting to ask for awhile as well. Thanks for asking Matthew, and thanks for doing the video on it, Robert!

replies.author: @rwells3376
replies.text: I would like to weigh in on your question if you don't mind. What you are asking is not simple, most people know very little of how complicated it is to produce reliable power. Of course you can look at solar and wind, but then you'll need an inverter, and I haven't seen those in the garbage much. Maybe a Stirling engine hooked up to an alternator, you can take out the diode pack and get A/C out of it. But them again you could design your home to run on DC and then the solar would be an option. No matter what you will have to come up with an energy source and hamsters on a wheel just won't work. I designed an off grid power system about ten years ago, it was ok but still had limitations, and mind you I didn't do this with items thrown out, they cost a small fortune. There isn't a day that goes by that I don't think of this, I firmly believe that in the future all homes will have their own power generation of some type. The person that makes just one thing work, will be a billionaire and be written into history. Good luck with your efforts.

replies.author: @ColRubyDimples
replies.text: @rwells3376 Thanks for that info! Personally, I'm willing to purchase things, but definitely want to keep the cost as low as possible, while being able to run what we want on the system. Something to start with and build onto over time is what I'm thinking. And as little expertise involved in its construction as possible. Tall order, for sure 😅

replies.author: @matthewgreen1152
replies.text: @ColRubyDimples the more you learn the more you can do and do it more simply. Study all of it that you can.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering On this subject, I'd like to offer an Idea I've had for a bit, now, but, first some background.
I'm sure you're familiar with Nuclear Isotope Batteries, where a container of isotopes is crammed together close enough to trigger _some_ fission, but not "Too much," and thermocouples to convert the heat to Zap.
My idea is a variant: If we can excite an electron with a Photon, and harvest it as power, why the bloody 'ell can't we do the same with α, ß, and Γ particles?
We could encase spent Nuclear fuel surrounded inside of a "Nucleo-Voltaic Panel."
Side benefit: Now there is no such thing as "Nuclear WASTE."
